Title: Cellulose biosynthesis and deposition in higher plants

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for proper cellulose synthesis in both primary and secondary cell walls; mutations reduce cellulose and can cause dwarfism, radial swelling, xylem collapse, or cytokinesis defects depending on allele strength.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KOR mutations reduce cellulose in both primary and secondary cell walls. 2. Strong alleles impair cytokinesis, whereas weaker alleles mainly affect secondary wall cellulose deposition. 3. KOR is not copurified with CesA complexes, so it is required for cellulose synthesis but is not an integral CesA complex subunit. 4. Its precise mechanistic role remains unresolved, with proposed functions in sterol-glucoside primer processing, microfibril editing, or chain termination.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Functions in both primary and secondary wall-forming tissues; associated with elongating and dividing cells and developing xylem based on mutant/localization studies
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Endosomes; Golgi membranes; motile compartments near plasma membrane; phragmoplast in dividing cells; localization at plasma membrane not consistently detected
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; complementation/localization with GFP fusion; heterologous enzyme assay showing cellulase activity; copurification tests with CesA complexes
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Localization reports are inconsistent. The review explicitly states that KOR is not an integral part of the cellulose synthase complex.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major structural component of plant cell walls; supports directional growth and mechanical strength
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong alleles cause cytokinesis defects and severe growth defects; weak alleles impair secondary wall formation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for altering cellulose assembly/extractability, but mechanistic uncertainty and severe developmental penalties in strong mutants limit direct deployment.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Primary cell wall of elongating root cells
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls oriented cellulose microfibril deposition during anisotropic cell expansion; cobra mutants show disorganized cellulose deposition and reduced cellulose.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COB encodes a polarly targeted GPI-anchored protein associated with elongating root cells. 2. Conditional cobra alleles showed that loss of ordered microfibril deposition precedes large cellulose reduction. 3. COB therefore functions mainly in orienting cellulose deposition rather than directly catalyzing cellulose synthesis.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Elongating root cells during anisotropic expansion
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane and longitudinal cell walls of elongating root cells; banded pattern perpendicular to growth axis
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant and conditional allele analysis; subcellular localization studies
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Distributed in a microtubule-dependent banded pattern. Reduced cellulose is interpreted as likely secondary to disordered deposition.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major structural component of plant cell walls; supports directional growth and mechanical strength
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Null alleles impair polarized cell elongation and root morphology
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for modifying wall architecture/orientation, but perturbation risks growth defects due to loss of anisotropic expansion.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal secondary cell wall cellulose synthesis; disruption causes irregular xylem phenotype and reduced cellulose.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCOBL4 was identified by transcriptomic coexpression with secondary wall CesAs. 2. T-DNA disruption caused an irregular xylem phenotype. 3. Mutants showed a significant decrease in cellulose content, supporting a direct role in secondary wall cellulose formation.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Coexpressed with secondary cell wall CesAs during stages of secondary wall formation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transcriptomic coexpression analysis; T-DNA mutant phenotyping; cellulose content measurement
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Rice ortholog had already been linked to secondary wall cellulose synthesis.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major structural component of secondary walls, contributing to tissue strength
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Irregular xylem phenotype indicates vascular mechanical defects when disrupted
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptomics/microarray coexpression datasets referenced; specific accession NA
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for engineering secondary wall architecture and biomass properties, though vascular integrity risks must be considered.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Predicted type II membrane protein of unknown function
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal cellulose accumulation and ordered cellulose microfibril orientation; mutants are dwarfed and cellulose deficient.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KOBITO mutants are cellulose deficient and dwarfed. 2. Recently deposited wall layers show randomized cellulose microfibril orientation in mutants. 3. The protein is required for proper cellulose deposition, but its precise biochemical role remains unknown.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Studied mainly in root zones associated with cell division and elongation
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Reported at plasma membrane in elongation zone; punctate intracellular/cytoplasmic pattern; cell wall localization also reported
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; microscopy of cellulose microfibril orientation; localization studies
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Different alleles were recovered as eld and abi8; link between ABA insensitivity and cellulose synthesis remains unclear.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major structural component of plant cell walls; supports directional growth and mechanical strength
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Dwarfism and broad growth defects when perturbed
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for wall organization, but unclear function and strong developmental defects limit utility.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase catalytic subunit; processive glycosyltransferase with D,D,D,QXXRW motif; RING-like zinc-binding domain; 8 transmembrane domains
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains that assemble into cellulose microfibrils at the plasma membrane.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key essential catalytic subunit for primary wall cellulose synthesis; rate-limiting not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for primary cell wall cellulose synthesis; severe/null alleles are embryo- or gametophyte-lethal, while rsw1 temperature-sensitive alleles cause cellulose deficiency and radial swelling.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCesA1 is an essential primary wall CesA. 2. The rsw1 mutation linked a CesA gene to rosette integrity and cellulose synthesis. 3. Null alleles are embryo- or gametophytic lethal, showing nonredundant function. 4. Together with AtCesA3 and a CesA6-related protein, it is required for primary wall cellulose synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Primary wall-forming tissues; also required during pollen development
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis including temperature-sensitive rsw1; freeze-fracture rosette analysis; genetic lethality studies; co-complex inference from primary wall CesA interaction studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The rsw1 allele caused rosette disassembly at restrictive temperature and accumulation of noncrystalline β-1,4-glucan.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major structural polysaccharide conferring strength and directional growth
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe alleles are embryo/gametophytic lethal; strong growth defects
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Core target for understanding and engineering primary wall cellulose, but essentiality makes strong perturbation impractical.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase catalytic subunit; processive glycosyltransferase with D,D,D,QXXRW motif; RING-like zinc-binding domain; 8 transmembrane domains
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains for cellulose microfibril formation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key essential catalytic subunit for primary wall cellulose synthesis; rate-limiting not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for primary wall cellulose synthesis; null alleles are gametophytically lethal, and viable alleles confer isoxaben resistance or trigger cellulose-defect responses.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCesA3 is a core nonredundant primary wall CesA. 2. Mutant alleles such as ixr1, cev1 and eli1 revealed links between cellulose deficiency and stress/lignification responses. 3. AtCesA3 interacts with AtCesA6 in the primary wall CesA complex, likely with AtCesA1. 4. Null alleles disrupt pollen development, showing essential function.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Primary wall-forming tissues; required in pollen development
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; isoxaben resistance screens; co-complex interaction studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Cellulose-deficient alleles activate ectopic lignification, jasmonate/ethylene signaling and disease-resistance-related responses.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major structural polysaccharide conferring strength and directional growth
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Null alleles are lethal in pollen; weaker alleles cause growth defects and ectopic defense/lignification responses
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important engineering target for cellulose synthesis/herbicide response, but strong perturbation causes severe developmental and defense-related tradeoffs.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase catalytic subunit; processive glycosyltransferase with D,D,D,QXXRW motif; RING-like zinc-binding domain; 8 transmembrane domains
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains for primary wall cellulose deposition.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key essential/substitutable primary wall catalytic subunit within CesA6-related group
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports primary wall cellulose synthesis and cell elongation; null mutants reduce root and hypocotyl elongation, and specific alleles confer isoxaben resistance.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCesA6 is required for normal primary wall cellulose synthesis and elongation. 2. It acts partially redundantly with AtCesA2, AtCesA5 and AtCesA9 depending on tissue. 3. AtCesA6 interacts with AtCesA3 in the primary wall cellulose synthase complex. 4. Mutation prc1-1 causes cellulose deficiency, while ixr2 confers isoxaben resistance.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Primary wall-forming tissues; roles differ by tissue and developmental stage; one of AtCesA2/6/9 required in pollen
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane cellulose synthase rosettes; YFP-CesA6 used to visualize moving complexes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; genetic redundancy studies; fluorescent protein imaging; protein interaction studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used in live-cell imaging to show cellulose synthase movement along cortical microtubule tracks.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major structural polysaccharide conferring strength and directional growth
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Isoxaben; light; low-dose oryzalin in imaging studies
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced elongation of roots/hypocotyls; seedling lethality in combination with AtCesA5 loss
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable for dissecting primary wall cellulose deposition and microtubule-guided synthesis; redundancy may allow nuanced engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase catalytic subunit; CesA6-related isoform
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Contributes to β-1,4-glucan polymerization as a CesA6-related primary wall cellulose synthase isoform.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; partially redundant auxiliary essential isoform in certain tissues
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Partially redundant with AtCesA6 in primary wall synthesis; knockout alone has mild phenotype but enhances defects when combined with AtCesA6 loss.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCesA2 belongs to the CesA6-related group. 2. Single mutants show little or mild cellulose-related phenotypes. 3. Double knockout with AtCesA6 enhances dwarfing and reduced elongation, indicating partial redundancy. 4. Along with AtCesA6 and AtCesA9, at least one member is required for proper pollen development.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Tissue-dependent contribution to primary wall synthesis and pollen development
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane cellulose synthase complex (inferred as CesA family member)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Phenotypic severity differs by Arabidopsis ecotype according to review discussion.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major structural polysaccharide conferring strength and directional growth
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: In double mutants with AtCesA6 causes stronger dwarfing and seedling defects
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Illustrates exploitable redundancy among primary wall CesAs, but effects are background dependent.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase catalytic subunit; CesA6-related isoform
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Contributes to β-1,4-glucan polymerization as a CesA6-related isoform in selected tissues/stages.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Partially redundant with AtCesA6; single mutants show little phenotype, but AtCesA5/6 double mutants are seedling lethal.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCesA5 is a CesA6-related primary wall cellulose synthase. 2. Single mutants show no clear cellulose-deficient phenotype. 3. Genetic redundancy with AtCesA6 is essential for seedling establishment.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Stage-specific/tissue-dependent contribution to primary wall synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review explicitly discusses its functional redundancy in the core primary wall CesA module.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major structural polysaccharide conferring strength and directional growth
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Double mutant with AtCesA6 is seedling lethal
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Represents redundancy node for fine-tuning cellulose deposition, but combined perturbation is lethal.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase catalytic subunit; CesA6-related isoform
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Contributes to β-1,4-glucan polymerization as a CesA6-related isoform in selected developmental contexts.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Partially redundant with AtCesA6-related group members; at least one of AtCesA2/6/9 is required for correct pollen development.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCesA9 belongs to the CesA6-related set supporting primary wall synthesis. 2. Single and double mutants show normal pollen development, but higher-order segregation demonstrates that one of AtCesA2/6/9 is required for proper pollen formation. 3. Its function is tissue-dependent and redundant.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Pollen development and other tissue-dependent primary wall contexts
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review assigns it as part of the CesA6-related functional redundancy group.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major structural polysaccharide conferring strength and directional growth
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss in combination with other CesA6-related genes impairs pollen development
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for understanding tissue-specific redundancy in cellulose synthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase catalytic subunit; processive glycosyltransferase with D,D,D,QXXRW motif; RING-like zinc-binding domain; 8 transmembrane domains
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains for secondary wall cellulose microfibrils.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key essential catalytic subunit for secondary wall cellulose synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for secondary cell wall cellulose deposition; mutations cause irregular xylem with collapsed vessels and major stem cellulose reduction.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCesA4 corresponds to irx5 and is essential for secondary wall cellulose synthesis. 2. Mutants retain only about 30% stem cellulose owing to loss of secondary wall cellulose. 3. AtCesA4 is coexpressed, colocalized and coassembled with AtCesA7 and AtCesA8 in the same complex. 4. All three subunits are nonredundant and necessary for proper complex assembly.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Developing xylem vessels and interfascicular fibres undergoing secondary wall formation
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane cellulose synthase rosettes in developing xylem elements
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; promoter-GUS and antibody localization; coimmunoprecipitation/copurification
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In absence of one secondary wall CesA subunit, remaining subunits fail to interact properly and accumulate intracellularly.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides mechanical strength and xylem function
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Collapsed xylem, reduced tissue strength and growth defects when disrupted
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target for biomass engineering because it directly controls secondary wall cellulose, but vascular integrity imposes constraints.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase catalytic subunit; processive glycosyltransferase with D,D,D,QXXRW motif; RING-like zinc-binding domain; 8 transmembrane domains
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains during secondary wall deposition.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key essential catalytic subunit for secondary wall cellulose synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for secondary wall cellulose synthesis; irx3/fra5 mutants show collapsed xylem, reduced fibre strength and strong cellulose loss.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCesA7 corresponds to irx3 and is indispensable for secondary wall cellulose deposition. 2. Functional epitope-tagged CesA7 enabled purification of a complex containing AtCesA4, AtCesA7 and AtCesA8. 3. Loss of one subunit abolishes proper interaction/localization of the others. 4. AtCesA7 is also subject to phosphorylation-associated proteasome-dependent degradation in vitro.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Developing xylem vessels and fibres undergoing secondary wall deposition
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; complementation with epitope-tagged protein; copurification/coimmunoprecipitation; colocalization; in vitro phosphorylation/degradation assays
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Protein degradation may be regulated by phosphorylation; ecotype background changes mutant severity.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides mechanical strength and xylem function
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe vascular collapse, sterility or growth reduction depending on ecotype
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Powerful handle for modifying secondary wall cellulose and biomass, but strong alleles severely impair vascular function and fertility.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase catalytic subunit; processive glycosyltransferase with D,D,D,QXXRW motif; RING-like zinc-binding domain; 8 transmembrane domains
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains during secondary wall cellulose deposition.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key essential catalytic subunit for secondary wall cellulose synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for secondary wall cellulose synthesis; irx1/fra6 mutants show collapsed xylem and reduced cellulose, although inactive protein can still support complex assembly.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCesA8 corresponds to irx1 and is essential for secondary wall cellulose synthesis. 2. Mutants have major cellulose loss specifically from secondary walls. 3. AtCesA8 interacts with AtCesA4 and AtCesA7 in the same complex. 4. An irx1-1 catalytic-site mutant protein appears assembly competent and is transported normally, implying activity is dispensable for complex assembly but not cellulose synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Developing xylem vessels and fibres undergoing secondary wall deposition
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Illustrates distinction between structural assembly and catalytic competence of the cellulose synthase complex.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides mechanical strength and xylem function
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Collapsed xylem and reduced strength when disrupted
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Relevant for engineering cellulose amount/quality and understanding catalytic versus assembly roles in rosettes.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains for secondary wall cellulose deposition in rice.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key essential catalytic subunit for secondary wall cellulose synthesis in rice
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation causes brittle culm phenotype with reduced tissue strength, consistent with reduced secondary wall cellulose.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OsCesA4 was identified through transposon mutations causing brittle culms. 2. It is an ortholog of Arabidopsis secondary wall CesAs. 3. The gene is required for normal tissue mechanical strength in rice.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Secondary wall-forming tissues such as culm-supporting cells (inferred from phenotype and orthology)
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: OsCesA7; OsCesA9 (functional ortholog set inferred from review)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review notes orthology relationships differ in numbering between Arabidopsis and rice.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides culm strength and structural support
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Brittle culm and reduced tissue strength when disrupted
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Relevant target for crop lodging resistance and biomass engineering in cereals.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains for secondary wall cellulose deposition in rice.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key essential catalytic subunit for secondary wall cellulose synthesis in rice
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation causes brittle culm phenotype and reduced tissue strength.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OsCesA7 is one of three rice CesAs identified by brittle culm mutants. 2. It functions as a rice ortholog of Arabidopsis secondary wall CesAs. 3. The gene is required for normal secondary wall strength.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Secondary wall-forming tissues such as culm-supporting cells (inferred)
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: OsCesA4; OsCesA9 (functional ortholog set inferred from review)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Ortholog of AtCesA4 according to review numbering comparison.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides culm strength and structural support
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Brittle culm and reduced strength when disrupted
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High relevance for cereal straw strength and biomass processing traits.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains for secondary wall cellulose deposition in rice.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key essential catalytic subunit for secondary wall cellulose synthesis in rice
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation causes brittle culm phenotype with reduced tissue strength.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OsCesA9 is a rice secondary wall CesA identified via brittle culm mutants. 2. It is orthologous to Arabidopsis secondary wall CesAs despite nonmatching numbering. 3. Loss of function compromises culm mechanical strength.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Secondary wall-forming tissues such as culm-supporting cells (inferred)
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: OsCesA4; OsCesA7 (functional ortholog set inferred from review)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Ortholog of AtCesA7 according to review numbering comparison.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides culm strength and structural support
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Brittle culm and reduced strength when disrupted
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Relevant for improving straw strength and tuning secondary wall biomass in rice.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Secondary cell wall of fibres and xylem
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activates secondary wall biosynthetic pathways, promoting cellulose deposition together with lignin and xylan; dominant repression reduces wall thickening and overexpression causes ectopic secondary walls.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SND1 is specifically expressed in interfascicular and xylem fibres. 2. Dominant repression drastically reduces secondary wall thickening in fibres. 3. Overexpression activates secondary wall biosynthetic pathways and ectopic deposition. 4. SND1 acts redundantly with NST1; MYB46 is a direct target.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Secondary cell wall biosynthetic pathways; MYB46
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Likely part of a transcriptional cascade; specific feedback not established
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Dominant repression; overexpression; expression analysis; target identification
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Master regulator of secondary wall formation in fibres
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Regulates cellulose in coordination with lignin and xylan pathways rather than cellulose alone.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Ectopic wall deposition or severe reduction of fibre secondary walls when misexpressed
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value engineering target to increase biomass/secondary wall deposition, but broad activation of lignin/xylan alongside cellulose may complicate processing.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Secondary cell wall of fibres and endothecium
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes secondary wall thickening; acts redundantly with SND1 in fibres and with SND2 in endothecium, affecting cellulose together with lignin and xylan deposition.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NST1 redundantly regulates secondary wall synthesis with SND1 in fibres. 2. Double knockout causes severe reduction in expression of secondary wall biosynthetic genes and loss of cellulose, lignin and xylan. 3. NST1 also acts redundantly with SND2 in endothecium, where secondary walls are needed for anther dehiscence.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Developmental program of fibre and anther differentiation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential redundant regulator of secondary wall cellulose deposition in specific tissues
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Controls the whole secondary wall program, not cellulose specifically.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural support and anther dehiscence-related wall thickening
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Misexpression causes ectopic secondary wall deposition; loss affects fertility/anther function and fibre development
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising for biomass improvement, but broad effects on reproductive tissues and wall composition require careful targeting.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes secondary wall thickening in endothecium redundantly with NST1; overexpression induces ectopic secondary wall deposition.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SND2 functions redundantly with NST1 in anther endothecium. 2. Double mutation eliminates secondary wall thickening in endothecium. 3. Overexpression of either factor causes ectopic secondary wall deposition.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Secondary cell wall biosynthetic genes in endothecium
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Tissue-specific regulator required for endothecium secondary wall deposition
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because review identifies it as part of transcriptional regulation of cellulose-containing secondary walls.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural thickening needed for anther rupture and pollen release
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss impairs anther dehiscence-related wall formation; misexpression causes ectopic walls
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: May be useful for tissue-specific wall engineering, especially reproductive tissues, but not a broad biomass target.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for endothecium secondary wall thickening; overexpression causes ectopic secondary wall formation.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB26 is essential for secondary wall thickening in the endothecium. 2. Overexpression triggers ectopic secondary wall formation. 3. It regulates cellulose-containing secondary walls in coordination with other wall polymers.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Secondary wall biosynthetic program in endothecium
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential tissue-specific activator of endothecium secondary wall formation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review does not define direct downstream cellulose genes.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Wall thickening needed for anther dehiscence
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Misexpression causes ectopic wall formation; loss likely affects fertility
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for targeted manipulation of reproductive wall traits, but broad deployment may affect fertility.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Secondary cell wall in fibres and xylem vessels
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes secondary wall thickening in fibres and xylem vessels; dominant repression abolishes normal thickening.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB46 is a direct target of SND1. 2. Dominant repression of MYB46 causes lack of secondary wall thickening in fibres and xylem vessels. 3. MYB46 acts as part of the transcriptional cascade coordinating cellulose, lignin and xylan synthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Fibres and xylem vessels undergoing secondary wall thickening
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important downstream switch in the SND1 secondary wall regulatory network
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review emphasizes coordinated control of cellulose with lignin and xylan.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural support and vascular wall thickening
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for tuning biomass deposition via downstream secondary wall activation.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mediates response to cellulose deficiency rather than cellulose synthesis itself; mutation suppresses growth defect and ectopic lignification of cellulose-deficient mutants without restoring cellulose levels.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. THE1 was identified as a suppressor of AtCesA6prc1-1. 2. Loss of THE1 attenuates growth defects and ectopic lignification in several cellulose-deficient mutants. 3. Overexpression enhances ectopic lignification in cellulose-deficient backgrounds. 4. THE1 acts in cell wall integrity signaling rather than restoring cellulose biosynthesis directly.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Cell wall defect response pathways leading to lignification and growth inhibition
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates response pathways in cellulose-deficient backgrounds
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Unknown signal generated specifically in cellulose-deficient mutants
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedback response to cell wall integrity defects
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Suppressor mutant analysis; overexpression in cellulose-deficient backgrounds
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller of wall-defect responses, not direct determinant of cellulose amount
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No phenotype in wild type upon mutation or overexpression, implying requirement for a deficiency-associated activating signal.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cellulose maintains wall integrity; THE1 mediates response when integrity is compromised
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Modulates lignification and growth inhibition under cellulose-deficient conditions
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to uncouple growth penalties from cellulose deficiency or wall engineering, though it does not restore cellulose levels directly.
